# Google Updates AI Search Guidelines: Three Changes Content Teams Need to Know

Google published a significant update to its AI optimization guide on June 20, 2026. The revision clarifies how the search engine evaluates content for its AI
Overview and Search Generative Experience features.

The update matters because AI Overviews now appear in roughly 35 % of search
results in the United States, up from 18 % a year ago, according to BrightEdge
tracking data from May 2026.

## What changed

The June 2026 update makes three substantive changes:

**No ideal page length.** The previous version included an example suggesting
that comprehensive content "may require 800 or more words." That example has
been removed. The new text reads: "There is no ideal page length for AI
ranking. Focus on answering the question completely."

> "We've seen too many content teams obsessing over word counts instead of
> answering questions. That's the behavior we're trying to correct."
>
> — Google Search Liaison Danny Sullivan, responding to questions on X
> following the update, June 21, 2026

**Freshness over permanence.** The guide now recommends a visible publication
date and a "last reviewed" date for any content that references time-sensitive
information. This applies especially to technical documentation, medical
advice, and financial guidance.

**Structured data alignment.** Google explicitly recommends that structured
data (JSON-LD, microdata) match the visible content on the page. Discrepancies
between schema markup and rendered text may be treated as a quality signal.

## What didn't change

- The core advice about answer-first structure remains intact.
- Recommendations against mandatory chunking and fixed thresholds are
  unchanged.
- The "E-E-A-T" framework still underpins the quality evaluation.

## What content teams should do now

First, audit your existing content for word-count padding. If a page hits 800
words because someone added a background section that doesn't answer a user
question, cut it.

Second, add publication and review dates to every page. The guide
specifically calls out undated technical documentation as a trustworthiness
concern.

Third, verify that your JSON-LD structured data matches what users see on
the page. Many teams generate schema from a CMS template and never check
whether the rendered output diverged.

## Sources

- [Google Search Central — AI optimization guide](https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/ai-optimization-guide)
- [BrightEdge — State of AI Search, May 2026](https://www.brightedge.com/research/ai-search-trends-2026)
- [Danny Sullivan X thread, June 21, 2026](https://x.com/dannysullivan/status/1804164278891234567)
